mnemonics.title: Cohen-Sutherland Region Codes
mnemonics.content: Remember bits as T-B-R-L (Top, Bottom, Right, Left) with values 8-4-2-1. Think: 'Tired Boys Rest Lazy' or use binary: 1000=8(T), 0100=4(B), 0010=2(R), 0001=1(L). Add values to get code.

mnemonics.title: Liang-Barsky Parameters
mnemonics.content: p values: 'Left Negative, Right Positive, Bottom Negative, Top Positive' - p1=-Δx, p2=Δx, p3=-Δy, p4=Δy. Remember: 'p for parallel, q for position'.

mnemonics.title: Trivial Accept/Reject
mnemonics.content: AND for reject (common outside region), OR for accept (at least one inside). 'AND equals zero OR equals zero: accept' - AERO. If neither, clip and compute intersections.

acronyms.usage: Line clipping algorithm using 4-bit region codes
Parametric line clipping algorithm for rectangular windows
Parametric algorithm for convex polygon clipping
Used to check trivial reject in Cohen-Sutherland (AND ≠ 0 means reject)
Used to check trivial accept in Cohen-Sutherland (OR = 0 means both inside)

visualTips.visualization: Draw clip window as a square divided into 9 regions. Label center 0000. Edges get 1,2,4,8. Corners get sum of adjacent edges. Color code: green for inside, red for outside regions.

Draw line segment as horizontal axis from t=0 to t=1. Show entry point t0 (maximum of lower bounds) and exit point t1 (minimum of upper bounds). Shaded region between 0-1 is original segment; clipped portion is between t0 and t1.
For each edge crossed, draw dashed perpendicular line from edge to line. Intersection point is where line crosses the boundary. Use similar triangles to calculate exact coordinates.

analogies.analogy: Like a postal sorting system where each bit represents a direction (north/south/east/west). A point gets 'stamps' for each direction it's beyond. If stamps match, reject; if none, accept.
Think of clipping as putting a rectangular picture frame (clip window) over a larger drawing and tracing only what shows through - everything outside the frame is cut away.
Think of t as percentage along the journey from point A to B. Clipping finds what percentage of the journey remains visible within the window boundaries.
